text:
The three-dimensional architecture of carbohelicenes has fascinated molecular chemists for many years, and these molecules have been widely used in many areas of science. Lately, their high circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) has also made them candidates for design of new optical materials. While enantioselective syntheses have been developed for several classes of fused carbo- and heterohelicenes, there is no direct catalytic method known for preparing the lower non-fused carbo[n]helicenes (n = 4–6).

We developed an efficient palladium-catalyzed enantioselective C–H arylation method for preparing lower carbo[n]helicenes using a bifunctional phosphine-carboxylate ligand. Mechanistic simulations suggested a complex model influencing the enantio-outcome of the reactions at the C–H activation and reductive elimination steps. In the latter stages, the enantio-induction is the result of a delicate balance between non-covalent (NC) interactions and the steric repulsions between the substrate and the ligand along the transition states of the two main reductive elimination pathways. Finally, we examined the photophysical and chiroptical properties of enantioenriched [n]helicenes, to gain some insight into the structural and optical relationships that exist among these molecules.
